facts.text: Bergenfield was incorporated on June 25, 1894, after settlers from Dutch and Huguenot backgrounds named the area Schraalenburgh, meaning “barren knoll.”
The New Jersey and New York Railroad built a station in 1873, but the town’s name stuck when officials printed “Bergen Fields” on tickets after residents couldn’t agree on a name.
During the Civil War, Bergenfield hosted a tannery, saw mill, chair factory, harness shop, shoemaking shop, and blacksmith, reflecting a diverse local industry.
The Cooper Chair Factory, built between 1841 and 1860, was the largest employer in Hackensack Township in 1860, hiring 25 workers.
The Schraalenburgh Dutch Reformed Church, constructed in the 1720s from local fieldstone, served as a communal hub for the town’s early agrarian settlers.
Bergenfield’s 1950s rock band, The Royal Teens, achieved national fame with the hit “Short Shorts,” putting the borough on the music map.
